questions.text: Identify the underlined clause. He tells a tale that sounds untrue.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A noun clause functions as a noun in a sentence. It could serve as a subject, object, or complement, but in this case, the clause does not act as a noun.
An adjective clause describes or modifies a noun in the sentence. The clause 'that sounds untrue' describes the noun 'tale', making this the correct answer.
An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb, providing information about time, place, condition, or reason. This is not the function of the clause in the sentence.
An independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence. The clause 'that sounds untrue' cannot stand alone, hence it is not an independent clause.

questions.explanation: “That sounds untrue” describes the noun tale, so it is an adjective clause.

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause.I do not know what he will do.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This is correct because the clause 'what he will do' functions as the object of the verb 'know’,answering the question of 'what'.
This is incorrect because an adjective clause modifies a noun, which is not the function of the clause in this sentence.
This is incorrect because an adverb clause modifies a verb, an adjective, or another adverb, which does not apply in this context.
This is incorrect as an independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence, while 'what he will do' cannot.

questions.explanation: A noun clause functions like a noun in a sentence. In this case, "what he will do" acts as the object of the verb "know." It fills the position where a noun would typically go to complete the meaning of the sentence ("I do not know something").

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause. He met a boy whose eyes were blue.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A noun clause functions as a noun in the sentence. The clause in the question does not serve this purpose, as it describes a noun instead.
An adjective clause modifies a noun, providing more information about it. In this sentence, 'whose eyes were blue' modifies 'boy', making this option correct.
An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb. The clause in question does not alter any verbs or adjectives, so it is not an adverb clause.
An independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence. The clause in question cannot stand alone, as it depends on the main clause, so it is not an independent clause.

questions.explanation: An adjective clause modifies a noun or pronoun by providing additional information about it. Here, "whose eyes were blue" describes the boy by specifying his eye color.

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause.Fatima, whose mom is a nurse, sits behind me in class.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A noun clause functions as a noun in the sentence but 'whose mom is a nurse' does not serve that purpose; instead, it describes Fatima.

An essential adjective clause provides necessary information to identify the noun it modifies. However, 'whose mom is a nurse' is not essential for identifying Fatima—it provides additional, non-essential information.
An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb. The clause 'whose mom is a nurse' does not modify any verb or action in the main clause.
This clause adds extra information about Fatima but is not crucial for understanding who she is; thus, it is non-essential.

questions.explanation: A non-essential adjective clause provides additional information about a noun or pronoun, but is not necessary to identify it. Here, the clause "whose mom is a nurse" gives extra information about Fatima, but it isn't essential to understand who she is in the context of the sentence.

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause.I teach English because I love it.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A noun clause functions as a noun within a sentence. It typically answers questions like 'what?' or 'who?'. In this case, 'because I love it' does not function as a noun, hence this option is incorrect.
An adjective clause describes a noun and usually begins with a relative pronoun. The clause 'because I love it' does not modify a noun, making this option incorrect.
An adverb clause modifies a verb, an adjective, or another adverb. 'Because I love it' tells us why the action of the main clause occurs, which qualifies it as an adverb clause.
An independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence. 'Because I love it' cannot stand alone, therefore, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb. In this case, "because I love it" modifies the verb "teach" by explaining the reason or cause for teaching.

questions.text: He helped him. Him is?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The direct object answers the question 'whom?' after the verb. In this case, 'helped' is the action, and 'him' is the direct object receiving the action.

An indirect object typically answers the question 'to whom?' or 'for whom?' the action is done. In this sentence, 'him' is not receiving the help indirectly; rather, he is the one being helped directly.
A clause is a group of words that contains a subject and a verb. 'Him' does not form a clause; it is a pronoun functioning as a noun in this context.
A complement completes the meaning of a subject or verb. In this case, 'him' is not providing additional information about the subject or verb; it is the direct object of the action.

questions.explanation: Receives the action of the verb directly. In the sentence, "him" is the direct object of the verb "helped," meaning it is the person who received the help.

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause .Which one is the person who stole your car?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This is the correct answer because the clause 'who stole your car' describes the noun 'person'. It provides more information about which person is being referred to.
An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb, answering questions like how, when, where, or why. This option is incorrect as the clause does not modify a verb in this context.
A noun clause functions as a subject, object, or complement in a sentence. This option is incorrect because 'who stole your car' does not function as a noun in this context.
This term is not standard in grammatical terminology. The clause in question is specifically an adjective clause, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: "Who stole your car" is an adjective clause because it modifies the noun "person", describes which person is being referred to, and begins with the relative pronoun "who".

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause. She kept walking, even though her feet were hurting.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A noun clause acts as a noun in the sentence, serving as a subject, object, or complement. The underlined clause does not serve these functions.
An adjective clause modifies a noun or pronoun in the main clause. The underlined clause does not describe or limit a noun or pronoun.
An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb, often indicating time, reason, or condition. The underlined clause provides a condition related to the action of walking.
A 'strong clause' is not a recognized grammatical term. The underlined clause does not fit this description.

questions.explanation: The clause even though her feet were hurting shows a contrast and explains under what condition she kept walking. Clauses that show time, reason, condition, contrast or manner are adverb clauses.

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause. We'll have the life that we wish.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: An adjective clause modifies a noun or pronoun. In this sentence, 'that we wish' describes 'the life' and specifies which life is being referred to. So, this option is correct.
An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb. This option is incorrect because there is no clause in the sentence that modifies a verb or provides information about how, when, or where.
A noun clause functions as a noun in a sentence. This option is incorrect because 'that we wish' does not serve as the subject, object, or complement in this context.
An independent clause expresses a complete thought and can stand alone. This option is incorrect as 'that we wish' cannot stand alone and depends on the main clause.

questions.explanation: "That we wish" is an adjective clause because it modifies the noun "life", begins with the relative pronoun "that", and describes the kind of life being referred to.

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause.It is important to ask whether the wedding is formal or semiformal.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: An adjective clause modifies a noun and usually starts with a relative pronoun like 'who', 'which', or 'that'. This option is incorrect because the given sentence does not contain a clause that describes a noun.

An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb and often begins with subordinating conjunctions like 'because', 'although', or 'if'. This option is incorrect because the clause in the question does not modify a verb or an adjective.

A noun clause can act as a subject, object, or complement in a sentence. In this case, 'whether the wedding is formal or semiformal' serves as the object of the verb 'ask'. This is the correct option because it correctly identifies the function of the clause in the sentence.
A main clause can stand alone as a complete sentence. This option is incorrect because the clause in question cannot stand alone and is part of a larger sentence.

questions.explanation: The clause “whether the wedding is formal or semi-formal” acts as the object of the verb ask, so it functions as a noun clause.

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause. My command is whatever you wish.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A noun clause acts as a noun within a sentence. In this case, 'whatever you wish' serves as the object of the verb 'is', making it a noun clause.
An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb. 'Whatever you wish' does not provide a description of an action; thus, it is not an adverb clause.
An adjective clause modifies a noun. 'Whatever you wish' does not modify a noun in this sentence, so it cannot be an adjective clause.
A preposition clause contains a preposition and typically provides information about direction, location, or time. The phrase 'whatever you wish' does not fit this definition, making it incorrect.

questions.explanation: "Whatever you wish" is a noun clause because it functions as the object of the sentence (the thing being commanded) begins with the relative pronoun "whatever", and refers to a thing or idea (the wish).

questions.text: You must wait here until I get back.The underlined clause is:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option suggests that the underlined clause can stand alone as a complete thought. However, this is incorrect because the clause relies on additional information to make sense.

This is the correct answer. A dependent clause cannot stand alone as a complete sentence and is introduced by a subordinating conjunction ('until' in this case), which indicates that it is reliant on the main clause.
This option refers to clauses that modify nouns or pronouns. The underlined clause does not serve this purpose, making this option incorrect.
This term is not commonly used in grammar. The clause in question is not functioning as a prepositional phrase, thus making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: A dependent clause (also known as a subordinate clause) cannot stand alone as a complete sentence. It depends on the main clause to provide context and meaning. "Until I get back" is a dependent clause because it cannot stand alone as a complete sentence and relies on the main clause.

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause. Whatever you decide is fine with me.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This clause acts as a noun in the sentence, serving as the subject or object. In this case, 'Whatever you decide' functions as the subject of the verb 'is'.
An adjective clause modifies a noun. However, 'Whatever you decide' does not describe or limit a noun in the sentence.
An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb. In this case, the clause does not provide information about how the action is performed, so it is not an adverb clause.
An independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence. However, 'Whatever you decide' cannot stand alone as it depends on the rest of the sentence.

questions.explanation: “Whatever you decide” acts as the subject of the sentence; therefore, it functions as a noun clause.

questions.text: I will be very surprised if I make the school soccer team.The underlined clause is:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option refers to a clause that can stand alone as a complete sentence. An independent clause expresses a complete thought and does not rely on any other clause.

This is the correct option. A dependent clause cannot stand alone as a complete sentence and is introduced by a subordinating conjunction (in this case, 'if'). It relies on an independent clause to provide meaning.
This option describes a dependent clause that modifies a noun. In this scenario, the underlined clause does not function to modify a noun, so this option is incorrect.
This term is misleading, as there is no specific 'preposition clause' in traditional grammar terminology. Prepositions typically start phrases, not clauses, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: A dependent clause cannot stand alone as a complete sentence and usually begins with a subordinating word like if, because, when, although, etc. The sentence: “If I make the school soccer team,” begins with “if” and cannot stand alone. It depends on the main clause “I will be very surprised” to make complete sense.

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause. That he believes his own story is remarkable.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This is correct because the clause 'that he believes his own story is remarkable' acts as a noun, functioning as the subject of a larger sentence or as a direct object.
This option is incorrect because an adjective clause modifies a noun or pronoun, and this clause does not serve that purpose.
This option is incorrect because an adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or other adverb, and the clause does not function in that way.
This option is incorrect as an independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence, while this clause cannot.

questions.explanation: The clause “That he believes his story” functions as a noun because it acts as a subject of the sentence. It does not describe a noun (adjective) or modify a verb (adverb), so it is not an adjective or adverb clause.

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause. You may sit wherever you like.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A noun clause functions as a noun in a sentence, typically serving as a subject or object. The phrase 'wherever you like' does not serve this function, as it does not act as a subject or object here.
An adjective clause modifies a noun and typically begins with a relative pronoun. The clause 'wherever you like' does not modify a noun but rather indicates a place, making this option incorrect.

An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb by answering questions like when, where, why, how, to what extent. The clause 'wherever you like' modifies the verb 'sit' by indicating the place, making this the correct answer.
An independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence. The phrase 'wherever you like' cannot stand alone and depends on the main clause for its meaning, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: "Wherever you like" modifies the verb "may sit" by telling us the place, so it's an adverb clause.

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause. I want to see what had happened.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This clause acts as a noun in the sentence, serving as the object of the verb 'see'.
This clause describes a noun, but in this case, it does not provide information about any noun in the sentence.
This clause modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb. However, the clause does not provide information about the manner, time, or place of 'see'.
This clause can stand alone as a complete sentence, but the given clause cannot stand independently without the main clause.

questions.explanation: "What had happened" functions as the object of "want to see," acting like a noun.

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause.After the movie was over, we went out to eat.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A noun clause acts as a noun in the sentence, such as the subject or object. The clause 'After the movie was over' does not function as a noun, so this option is incorrect.
An essential adjective clause modifies a noun and provides necessary information. In this case, the clause does not describe or modify a noun directly, making this option incorrect.

An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb by providing information such as time, place, condition, or reason. The clause 'After the movie was over' indicates when the action of going out to eat occurred, which makes this option correct.

A non-essential clause provides additional information that can be removed without changing the main meaning of the sentence. The clause 'After the movie was over' is essential to understanding when the action took place, so this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: “After the movie was over” is an adverb clause because it tells when we went out to eat. It modifies the verb “went” and is not acting as a noun or describing a noun.

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause:The food that she cooked was delicious.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A noun clause functions as a subject, object, or complement in a sentence. In this case, 'that she cooked' does not serve as a noun.
An adjective clause modifies a noun or pronoun by providing more information about it. Here, 'that she cooked' describes 'the food', making it an adjective clause.
An adverb clause modifies verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs, providing context such as time, reason, or manner. 'That she cooked' does not modify a verb here.
An independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence. The phrase 'that she cooked' cannot stand alone, as it relies on the main clause for meaning.

questions.explanation: “That she cooked” modifies the noun food, giving more information about it. Thus, it is an adjective clause.

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause.If they could love like you and me, imagine what the world could be.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: An adjective clause modifies a noun or pronoun in a sentence. In this case, the clause does not provide descriptive information about a noun.

An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb by providing additional information such as time, reason, or condition. The clause in the sentence serves to explain the hypothetical scenario, making it the correct answer.
A noun clause functions as a subject, object, or complement in a sentence. The given clause does not serve any of these functions.
A main clause can stand alone as a complete sentence. The given clause is part of a larger structure and does not function as a complete thought by itself.

questions.explanation: "If they could love like you and me" is an adverb clause modifying the verb "imagine," expressing a condition.

questions.text: Identify the type of clause in the following sentence:I will call you when I reach home.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A noun clause functions as a noun within a sentence, but in this case, 'when I reach home' does not serve that role.
An adjective clause modifies a noun or pronoun, but 'when I reach home' does not describe a noun in this sentence.

An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb and provides information about time, place, condition, or reason. Here, 'when I reach home' modifies the verb 'will call' by indicating the time of the action.
An independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence, but 'when I reach home' cannot function independently.

questions.explanation: “When I reach home” tells us when the action in the main clause happens. Clauses that give information about time, place, reason, condition, etc are adverb clauses.

questions.text: Identify the clause in the following sentence:That she won the prize surprised everyone.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This clause acts as a noun in the sentence, serving as the subject of the verb 'surprised'. It answers the question of what surprised everyone.
An adjective clause modifies a noun and provides additional information about it. In this sentence, there is no noun being modified by a clause.
An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb, explaining when, where, why, or how something happened. This sentence does not contain such a clause.
An independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence. The clause identified here cannot stand alone, as it is dependent on the main clause.

questions.explanation: The clause “That she won the prize” acts as the subject of the sentence. Clauses that function as a noun (subject, object, or complement) are noun clauses.

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause. Whoever smelt it dealt it.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A noun clause acts as a subject or object within a sentence. In this case, 'Whoever smelt it' functions as the subject of the sentence.
An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb. 'Whoever smelt it' does not serve this function in the sentence.
An adjective clause modifies a noun. The clause in question does not describe or limit a noun, so it's not an adjective clause.
A preposition clause is not a recognized type of clause in English grammar. The clause here does not function as a prepositional phrase.

questions.explanation: "Whoever smelt it" is a noun clause because it: Functions as the subject of the sentence. Begins with the relative pronoun “whoever”.Refers to a person or entity.

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause. It is a light thing for whoever keeps his foot outside trouble to advise and counsel him who suffers.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is correct as the clause 'whoever keeps his foot outside trouble' acts as a noun, serving as the subject of the sentence.
An adverb clause modifies verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs, but in this case, the clause does not provide any modification to a verb or adjective.
An adjective clause describes a noun, but the clause here does not modify any noun in the sentence directly.
There is no such thing as a preposition clause; clauses function differently than phrases and are not categorized this way.

questions.explanation: The part “for whoever keeps his foot outside trouble” is a noun clause because it functions as the object of the preposition “for.” It tells “for whom” it is light to advise and counsel. A noun clause often acts as a subject, object, or complement in a sentence, which is the case here.

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause.She sings as though she were a professional.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A noun clause functions as a subject, object, or complement within a sentence. In this case, the clause does not serve any of those roles.
An adjective clause modifies a noun or pronoun, providing more information about it. This clause does not modify any noun in the sentence.

An adverb clause modifies a verb, an adjective, or another adverb by providing additional context such as time, reason, or manner. The clause 'as though she were a professional' describes the manner in which she sings, making it an adverb clause.
An independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence. The clause in question cannot stand alone; it relies on the main clause for meaning.

questions.explanation: “As though she were a professional” modifies the verb sings, telling how she sings. Therefore, it is an adverb clause.

questions.text: Identify the underlined clause.I will not make the same mistakes that you did.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: An adjective clause modifies a noun or pronoun. In this sentence, 'that you did' describes 'mistakes', indicating it is providing more information about which mistakes are being referred to.
An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb. The clause 'that you did' does not perform this function, as it does not describe how, when, or where something happened.

A noun clause functions as a subject, object, or complement in a sentence. 'That you did' does not replace a noun or serve as a subject or object; instead, it gives additional information about the mistakes.
A principal clause can stand alone as a complete sentence. The main clause in this sentence is 'I will not make the same mistakes', while 'that you did' is dependent and cannot stand alone.

questions.explanation: The clause “that you did” describes or modifies the noun “mistakes”, so it functions as an adjective clause.

questions.text: I usually do my homework while my mother is cooking dinner. The underlined clause is:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because an independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence, which is not the case here.
This option is correct because the clause 'while my mother is cooking dinner' cannot stand alone as a sentence and depends on the main clause for its meaning.
This option is incorrect; an adjective clause modifies a noun and usually begins with a relative pronoun, which is not the case here.
This option is incorrect because there is no such classification as a 'preposition clause'; prepositions typically introduce phrases, not clauses.

questions.explanation: "While my mother is cooking dinner" is a dependent clause because it provides additional information about the timing of the action in the main clause and cannot stand alone as a complete sentence.

questions.text: Identify the clause: While I was sleeping, the phone rang.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A noun clause functions as a subject, object, or complement in a sentence. The clause 'While I was sleeping' does not fulfill this role, as it acts to provide context rather than serve as a noun.
An adjective clause modifies a noun or pronoun. The clause 'While I was sleeping' does not describe a noun; instead, it gives information about the timing of the action in the main clause.
An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb. The clause 'While I was sleeping' provides information about when the phone rang, making it an adverb clause.
An independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence. 'While I was sleeping' cannot stand alone, thus it is not an independent clause.

questions.explanation: “While I was sleeping” modifies the verb rang by indicating when it happened, so it is an adverb clause.

questions.text: "She enjoys reading books because it relaxes her." The underlined clause is:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. A dependent clause cannot stand alone and relies on an independent clause to provide complete meaning.
This option is incorrect. An independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence, which is not the case here.
This option is incorrect. In the given sentence, only one clause is independent; the other is dependent.
This option is incorrect. The sentence contains one dependent clause and one independent clause.

questions.explanation: “Because it relaxes her” cannot stand alone as a complete sentence, so it is a dependent clause.

questions.text: He did his homework and went to bed when I reached home. The underlined clause is:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because an independent clause expresses a complete thought and can stand alone. The clause 'when I reached home' cannot stand alone and therefore is not independent.
This is the correct option because 'when I reached home' cannot stand alone as a complete thought. It relies on the main clause 'He did his homework and went to bed' to provide context.
This option is incorrect because an adjective clause modifies a noun and provides descriptive information. The clause in question does not modify a noun; it indicates a time relation.
This option is incorrect as there is no such thing as a 'preposition clause.' Prepositions can be part of phrases, but they do not form clauses on their own.

questions.explanation: The part "when I reached home" cannot stand alone as a complete sentence. It depends on the main clause to make sense.
